summary: Jeff Atwood argues that Apple's switch to Intel x86 CPUs has fundamentally changed the value proposition of Mac laptops. After years of Apple charging premium prices for underpowered PowerPC machines backed by dubious 'supercomputer on a chip' marketing, the new Intel-based MacBooks are now price-competitive or even cheaper than equivalent Windows laptops. He provides multiple price comparisons showing MacBooks undercutting Dell, Asus, and Fujitsu alternatives with similar specs. Atwood concludes that with Boot Camp enabling Windows compatibility, Apple laptops have become compelling options even for dedicated Windows users.
key_points: Apple's switch from PowerPC to Intel x86 CPUs made Macs genuinely competitive with PCs in performance because they literally became PCs
The new MacBook and MacBook Pro laptops are often $200-700 cheaper than Windows laptops with equivalent specifications
Apple's strategy has shifted from the old premium-pricing approach to the iPod-style market share strategy of competitive pricing
Boot Camp enables running Windows on Mac hardware, though some usability kinks like right-click support still need work
Apple's design quality gives them an edge even when prices are comparable to Windows alternatives
money_quotes: The new Apple models really are as fast as PCs – because they are PCs.
Forget the reality distortion field advertising. Forget the fact that Apple just obsoleted their entire PC line overnight. Again.
iPods are not only competitively priced, they're often cheaper. Today's Apple is very different than the old Apple, but many people still haven't caught on.
The current crop of x86 Apple laptops are cheaper than nearly every other x86 laptop of equivalent spec.
Now you can get a MacBook cheaper than most of its PC rivals.
The rare few laptops that are comparable in price and specification can't compete with Apple's design chops.
My next wintel laptop might just be an Apple.
key_insight: Apple's Intel transition transformed MacBooks from overpriced status symbols into genuinely cost-competitive machines that beat Windows laptops on both price and design.
